2. **Reduced immunogenicity:** The PEG steric shield masks antigenic epitopes, lowering antibody-mediated clearance and hypersensitivity reactions (e.g., Pegaspargase, PEG-ADA).

*(Third advantage for completeness: improved solubility and reduced aggregation, enhanced thermal and pH stability.)*

**Mark allocation:** Definition (1) + 2 advantages (1 each).

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**What are Locked Nucleic Acids (LNA)? Mention their key structural feature and one application.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

LNA (Locked Nucleic Acid) is a conformationally restricted RNA analogue in which the ribose sugar is **locked in the C3'-endo (N-type) conformation** by a methylene bridge connecting the 2'-oxygen to the 4'-carbon (2'-O,4'-C-methylene linkage).

**Key structural feature:** The 2'-O,4'-C methylene bridge — locks the furanose ring, pre-organizes the oligonucleotide for A-form duplex binding, leading to dramatically increased thermal stability of duplexes.

**Application:** Used as antisense oligonucleotides for miRNA inhibition in hepatitis C therapy (e.g., Miravirsen — anti-miR-122 LNA-modified ASO) and as probes in fluorescence in situ hybridization (FISH) for cytogenetic diagnostics (e.g., HER2/neu amplification detection in breast cancer).

**Mark allocation:** Definition (1) + structural feature (1) + application (1).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks): PEGylation of Therapeutic Enzymes**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper Pattern]**

**(a)** With neat reaction schemes, explain the NHS-ester route of PEGylation of a therapeutic enzyme. Discuss the role of DCC and HOSu in this process. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the pharmacological benefits of PEGylated L-asparaginase (Pegaspargase) over native L-asparaginase. How does PEG molecular weight influence the pharmacokinetic profile? **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** PEG is first converted to a PEG-carboxylic acid derivative: HO-PEG-OH → HOOC-PEG-COOH (oxidation) or HO-PEG-OH → HO-PEG-COOH (selective oxidation). This introduces a terminal carboxylic acid handle.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Activation of the carboxylic acid using **DCC (N,N'-dicyclohexylcarbodiimide)** and **HOSu (N-hydroxysuccinimide)** in anhydrous DMF or DCM at 0–4 °C:

$$ \text{HOOC-PEG-R} + \text{HOSu} + \text{DCC} \longrightarrow \text{R-PEG-COOSu} + \text{DCU} \downarrow $$

- **DCC** acts as a dehydration/coupling agent, activating the carboxyl group by forming an O-acylisourea intermediate.
- **HOSu** displaces the O-acylisourea to form a more stable, water-tolerant NHS-ester, while DCU (dicyclohexylurea) precipitates out and is filtered.
- The NHS-ester is now a strong electrophile, selectively attacked by nucleophilic Lys $-\text{NH}_2$ groups on the enzyme.

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Coupling of NHS-PEG to enzyme Lys residues (pH 7.5–9.0, 4 °C overnight):

$$ \text{Enzyme} - \text{NH}_2 + \text{R-PEG-COOSu} \longrightarrow \text{Enzyme} - \text{NH}-\text{CO}-\text{PEG}-\text{R} + \text{HOSu} $$

Stable amide bond is formed. HOSu is washed off during dialysis/ultrafiltration.

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Purification by size-exclusion chromatography (SEC) separates mono-, di-, and unreacted species. Quality control by SDS-PAGE (shift in band), MALDI-TOF (mass increase), and RP-HPLC.

**Why DCC and HOSu together?** DCC alone gives an unstable O-acylisourea that rapidly racemizes and hydrolyzes. HOSu converts it to a more stable NHS-ester with longer shelf life, better selectivity, and less side reactions.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Native L-asparaginase (from *E. coli*) is an enzyme used to treat Acute Lymphoblastic Leukemia (ALL). It hydrolyzes plasma asparagine, depriving leukemic cells of this essential amino acid. However:
- Short half-life ($t_{1/2} \approx 24$ h in humans).
- High immunogenicity — patients develop anti-asparaginase antibodies, leading to hypersensitivity and rapid clearance.
- Requires frequent dosing (2–3 times/week).

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Pegaspargase = PEG-conjugated (typically 5 kDa PEG chains, multi-PEGylated) form. Pharmacological benefits:
- $t_{1/2}$ extended to **5–14 days** (5–14× longer).
- Reduced immunogenicity — PEG mask prevents antibody recognition.
- Once-per-treatment-cycle dosing in chemotherapy regimens.
- Reduced anaphylaxis risk.

**Step 3 (3 marks):** **PEG MW vs. pharmacokinetic profile:**

| PEG MW | Effect on $R_h$ | Half-life Extension | Activity Retention | Notes |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---|
| 5 kDa | Small increase | 2–5× | 70–85% | Good activity balance |
| 20 kDa | Large increase | 10–30× | 50–70% | Best $t_{1/2}$ balance |
| 40 kDa | Very large | 30–100× | 30–50% | Risk of steric blocking of active site |

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Trade-off — **larger PEG = longer $t_{1/2}$ but greater activity loss** due to steric hindrance at the active site. Optimal PEG MW chosen by balancing half-life vs. retained catalytic activity. For asparaginase, 5 kDa branched PEG is commonly used.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks): Modification of Nucleic Acids**

**[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper Pattern]**

**(a)** With suitable structures, describe the chemistry of **phosphorothioate (PS) modification** and **2'-O-methyl modification** of oligonucleotides. Explain how each confers nuclease resistance. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is **SELEX**? Describe the steps involved in selecting DNA/RNA aptamers. List two therapeutic aptamers and their indications. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** **Phosphorothioate (PS) Modification:**
- The internucleotidic phosphodiester linkage ($P=O$) has one non-bridging oxygen replaced by sulfur ($P=S$).
- Synthesized during solid-phase oligonucleotide synthesis by replacing iodine oxidation with **Beaucage reagent** or **DDTT** as the sulfur-transfer agent.

$$ \text{P(III)} \xrightarrow{\text{Beaucage reagent}} \text{P=S} $$

- Stereochemistry: PS creates a chiral center at phosphorus → Rp and Sp diastereomers.

**Step 2 (1.5 marks):** **Nuclease resistance mechanism:**
- Native phosphodiester is negatively charged and recognized by nucleases (e.g., snake venom PDE, S1 nuclease).
- Sulfur substitution slightly increases bond length (P=S ≈ 1.95 Å vs P=O ≈ 1.48 Å) and changes the electronic environment, **sterically and electronically hindering nuclease access**.
- Result: PS-modified oligos exhibit 10–20× higher stability in serum.

**Step 3 (2 marks):** **2'-O-methyl (2'-OMe) modification:**
- Methyl group attached to the 2'-oxygen of the ribose sugar: $\text{Ribose-2'-O-CH}_3$.
- Synthesized using 2'-OMe-phosphoramidite monomers in solid-phase synthesis.
- Retains RNA-like sugar puckering (C3'-endo), so it mimics RNA in duplex geometry.

**Step 4 (1.5 marks):** **Nuclease resistance mechanism:**
- The 2'-O-methyl ether eliminates the 2'-OH that is the nucleophile attacking the phosphodiester in the nuclease catalytic mechanism.
- Also, the methyl group provides steric bulk that blocks nuclease binding.
- 2'-OMe increases serum stability by 5–10×, often combined with PS in "gapmer" ASO design.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 (1.5 marks):** **SELEX Definition:**
**SELEX (Systematic Evolution of Ligands by EXponential Enrichment)** is an iterative *in vitro* selection technique used to identify single-stranded DNA or RNA sequences (**aptamers**) that bind a target molecule with high affinity and specificity, starting from a vast random-sequence library.

**Step 2 (3 marks):** **SELEX Steps:**

1. **Library design:** A random ssDNA library of 10¹³–10¹⁵ unique 40–80 nt sequences, flanked by fixed primer-binding regions (5' and 3').
2. **Incubation:** Library is incubated with the **target** (protein, small molecule, cell surface) under defined buffer/temperature conditions.
3. **Partitioning:** Bound sequences are separated from unbound via nitrocellulose filter, affinity column, magnetic beads, or capillary electrophoresis (CE-SELEX).
4. **Elution:** Bound aptamers are eluted (denaturation, ligand displacement, or proteolytic release).
5. **Amplification:** Eluted DNA is PCR-amplified (with biotinylated primers for ssDNA regeneration) or RNA is reverse-transcribed and PCR-amplified.
6. **Iteration:** Steps 2–5 are repeated 8–15 rounds, with counter-selection against negative targets in alternate rounds.
7. **Cloning & Sequencing:** Final pool is cloned into *E. coli*, sequenced, and individual aptamers characterized.
8. **Characterization:** $K_d$ measured by SPR, ITC, or fluorescence anisotropy.

**Step 3 (1.5 marks):** **Two Therapeutic Aptamers:**

| Aptamer | Target | Indication | Modification |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| **Pegaptanib (Macugen)** | VEGF-165 isoform | Age-related Macular Degeneration (AMD) | 2'-F pyrimidines, 2'-OMe purines, 3'-idT cap, 40 kDa PEG |
| **Defibrotide** | ssDNA mixture, anti-thrombotic | Hepatic Veno-Occlusive Disease (VOD) | Natural ssDNA from porcine gut |

**Additional emerging aptamers (for reference):** NOX-A12 (CXCL12, oncology), NOX-E36 (CCL2, diabetic kidney disease).

**Step 4 (1 mark):** **Advantages of aptamers over antibodies:**
- Fully synthetic (no batch-to-batch variability).
- Smaller (~15–25 kDa) → better tissue penetration.
- Reversible denaturation → easy regeneration.
- Modifiable chemistry → fine-tuned pharmacokinetics.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting mass balance** in PEGylation yield problems → losing 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Confusing NHS-ester with imine** — clearly distinguish amide (stable) from Schiff base (reversible, must be reduced).
> 3. **Missing the difference between DS (degree of substitution) and yield** — DS = bound/available, Yield = obtained/theoretical.
> 4. **In SELEX answers, students often forget counter-selection** — explicitly mention negative selection rounds to avoid nonspecific binders.
> 5. **In LNA/PNA questions, do not forget the structural lock or backbone neutrality** — examiners specifically test structural awareness.
> 6. **For 2'-OMe and 2'-F, do not interchange** — 2'-F is electronegative (hydrogen bond mimic), 2'-OMe is steric (methyl ether).
> 7. **PS modification retains negative charge** (S replaces O, not OH) — students incorrectly write P=O → P-OH thinking sulfur adds an OH.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **🎯 High-Density Revision Bullets — KTU Module 3:**

- ✅ **PEGylation** = covalent PEG attachment → ↑ $t_{1/2}$, ↓ immunogenicity, ↑ solubility.
- ✅ **NHS-ester route** uses DCC + HOSu; targets Lys $-\text{NH}_2$; forms stable amide.
- ✅ **Maleimide route** targets Cys $-\text{SH}$; forms stable thioether.
- ✅ **Aldehyde route** (periodate/glutaraldehyde) targets Lys; forms Schiff base → reduce with NaBH$_3$CN.
- ✅ **Cross-linking**: Glutaraldehyde forms Schiff base → NaBH$_4$ reduction to stable amine.
- ✅ **CLEAs** = Cross-linked Enzyme Aggregates; CLEs = Cross-linked Enzyme Crystals.
- ✅ **Dextran/chitosan conjugation** improves stability, mucoadhesion, biocompatibility.
- ✅ **PS modification**: P=O → P=S, nuclease resistance, used in antisense drugs.
- ✅ **2'-O-methyl**: methyl ether on 2'-OH, blocks nuclease mechanism, mimics RNA conformation.
- ✅ **2'-F**: fluorine on 2' position, electronegative, mimics 2'-OH in H-bonding.
- ✅ **LNA**: 2'-O,4'-C methylene bridge, locks C3'-endo, highest $T_m$ gain per modification.
- ✅ **PNA**: peptide-like amide backbone, charge-neutral, protease + nuclease immune.
- ✅ **5'-phosphorylation** by T4 polynucleotide kinase (PNK), important for ligation.
- ✅ **3'-end labeling** by TdT + modified dUTP, or periodate + hydrazide chemistry.
- ✅ **Biotin-NHS** couples to Lys $-\text{NH}_2$; binds streptavidin ($K_d \approx 10^{-14}$ M).
- ✅ **SELEX** = 8–15 rounds of selection; counter-selection removes nonspecific binders.
- ✅ **Aptamers** = antibody analogues (15–25 kDa), $K_d$ in pM–nM range.
- ✅ **Pegaptanib** = PEGylated anti-VEGF RNA aptamer for AMD; first FDA-approved aptamer (2004).
- ✅ **Engineering tip:** PEG MW trade-off — larger PEG = ↑ $t_{1/2}$ but ↓ activity.
- ✅ **Gapmer design**: 2'-OMe flanks + PS central DNA gap → RNase H recruitment.
- ✅ **mRNA modification**: N1-methylpseudouridine replaces uridine to evade innate immunity.
- ✅ **Degree of Substitution (DS)** is a critical QC parameter; over-modification = activity loss.
- ✅ **Stability hierarchy for nucleic acid analogues:** PNA > LNA > PS-DNA > 2'-OMe-RNA > DNA.
- ✅ **For 14-mark answers**, always include: **reaction scheme + mechanism + application + example drug + clinical relevance**.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Properties of Common Enzymes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBB866 - BIOCONJUGATE TECHNOLOGY & APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Properties of Common Enzymes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Bioconjugate Technology**, an **enzyme** is a macromolecular biological catalyst—almost always a protein (or a catalytically active RNA molecule, i.e., a ribozyme)—that accelerates a specific biochemical transformation by lowering the activation energy barrier of the reaction. When an enzyme is covalently or non-covalently coupled to another biomolecule (antibody, nucleic acid, polymer, nanoparticle, or solid support), the resulting hybrid construct is referred to as a **bioconjugate**, and the enzyme component confers **catalytic signal amplification**, **substrate specificity**, and **turnover-based signal generation**.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** framework (Course PEBB866), the "properties of common enzymes" refers to the catalogue of physico-chemical, kinetic, and functional descriptors that govern the **selection, bioconjugation strategy, and analytical performance** of enzymes routinely employed in diagnostics, biosensing, therapeutics, and industrial biocatalysis.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Enzymes in bioconjugate technology are evaluated on the basis of their (i) catalytic efficiency, (ii) substrate specificity, (iii) stability profile, (iv) availability of reactive functional groups (–NH₂, –COOH, –SH, carbohydrate, imidazole), (v) cofactor dependence, and (vi) amenability to site-directed chemical or genetic modification.

## 1.2 Core Functional Role in Bioconjugation

Enzymes used in bioconjugate technology typically perform one of three functions:

1. **Reporter / Signal-Generating Role** — e.g., Horseradish Peroxidase (HRP), Alkaline Phosphatase (AP), β-Galactosidase, Glucose Oxidase (GOx). These enzymes convert a colourless substrate into a coloured, fluorescent, luminescent, or electrochemical product.
2. **Cross-linking / Assembly Role** — e.g., Transglutaminase, Sortase A, Lysyl Oxidase, HaloTag/SNAP-tag ligases. These enzymes catalyse the formation of covalent bonds between two bioconjugate partners.
3. **Cleavage / Activation Role** — e.g., Trypsin, Chymotrypsin, TEV protease, Thrombin, Factor Xa. These enzymes hydrolyse specific peptide bonds, releasing or activating a conjugate.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Molecular Specialist Worker":**
> Imagine a factory assembly line where every general worker (a non-enzymatic catalyst) can do *anything but nothing brilliantly*. Now place a **specialist craftsman** at one station — he has a custom-fitted toolbelt, a single dedicated workshop, and a hidden pocket of energy he can deploy on demand. He performs *one job* but does it **millions of times faster** than anyone else, with **zero waste**. That craftsman is your enzyme.
> In the bioconjugate factory, when we **pin his toolbelt to an antibody or a nanoparticle** (i.e., conjugate the enzyme), we essentially turn him into a **mobile detection unit** — wherever the antibody docks, he instantly reports the event by producing a visible/fluorescent signal.

## 1.4 The "Common" Designation

The descriptor **"common"** in the topic title is syllabus-specific. It refers to a recurring panel of approximately **12–15 well-characterised enzymes** that dominate published bioconjugate literature and KTU examination questions:

| S.No. | Enzyme | Primary Bioconjugate Role |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| 1 | Horseradish Peroxidase (HRP) | Reporter (colorimetric / chemiluminescent) |
| 2 | Alkaline Phosphatase (AP) | Reporter (colorimetric / chemiluminescent) |
| 3 | β-Galactosidase | Reporter (fluorogenic) |
| 4 | Glucose Oxidase (GOx) | Reporter / Biosensor |
| 5 | Catalase | Signal quench / Payload release |
| 6 | Trypsin | Cleavage |
| 7 | Chymotrypsin | Cleavage |
| 8 | Lysozyme | Antimicrobial conjugate |
| 9 | Carbonic Anhydrase | Prodrug activation / Targeting |
| 10 | L-Asparaginase | Therapeutic (anticancer conjugate) |
| 11 | Urease | pH-responsive biosensor |
| 12 | Transglutaminase (TG2 / mTG) | Cross-linking |
| 13 | Sortase A | Site-specific ligation |
| 14 | Penicillin G Acylase (PGA) | Prodrug activation |
| 15 | HaloTag / SNAP-tag | Self-labelling fusion |

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical Tip:** For KTU examinations, master the **first six enzymes in detail**. HRP, AP, β-Gal, GOx, Trypsin, and Lysozyme collectively account for ~85% of all Part A and Part B questions in Module 3.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Classification of Common Enzymes

Enzymes are formally classified by the **IUBMB Enzyme Commission (EC)** system into **7 major classes**. Every common enzyme used in bioconjugation can be mapped onto this scheme.

### EC Class 1 — Oxidoreductases

Catalyse **oxidation–reduction** reactions using cofactors such as NAD(P)⁺, FAD, or heme.

- **HRP (EC 1.11.1.7)** — Uses **heme** (ferriprotoporphyrin IX) as prosthetic group; reduces H₂O₂ to water while oxidising aromatic substrates (TMB, ABTS, OPD, luminol).
- **GOx (EC 1.1.3.4)** — Flavoenzyme; oxidises β-D-glucose to D-glucono-δ-lactone + H₂O₂.

### EC Class 2 — Transferases

Catalyse transfer of functional groups between donor and acceptor.

- **Transglutaminase (EC 2.3.2.13)** — Transfers an acyl group from a glutamine residue to a primary amine (e.g., lysine ε-amino group), forming an **isopeptide bond (γ-carboxamide)**.

### EC Class 3 — Hydrolases

Catalyse bond cleavage using **water**.

- **Alkaline Phosphatase (EC 3.1.3.1)** — Hydrolyses phosphate monoesters. **Zn²⁺-metalloenzyme** (two Zn²⁺ + one Mg²⁺ per active site).
- **β-Galactosidase (EC 3.2.1.23)** — Hydrolyses β-1,4 glycosidic bond of lactose.
- **Trypsin (EC 3.4.21.4)** — **Serine protease**; cleaves C-terminal to Lys and Arg (with Pro exclusion).
- **Chymotrypsin (EC 3.4.21.1)** — **Serine protease**; cleaves C-terminal to aromatic residues (Phe, Trp, Tyr).
- **Lysozyme (EC 3.2.1.17)** — Hydrolyses β-1,4 bond between *N*-acetylmuramic acid (NAM) and *N*-acetylglucosamine (NAG) in bacterial peptidoglycan.

### EC Class 4 — Lyases

Cleave bonds by mechanisms **other than hydrolysis or oxidation** (e.g., addition/elimination).

- **Carbonic Anhydrase (EC 4.2.1.1)** — **Zn²⁺-metalloenzyme**; reversibly hydrates CO₂ → HCO₃⁻.

### EC Class 5 — Isomerases

Catalyse geometric or structural rearrangements.

- *(Rare in bioconjugation; typically not examined in Module 3.)*

### EC Class 6 — Ligases

Catalyse bond formation coupled to **ATP hydrolysis**.

- **Sortase A (EC 6.3.2.??)** — Recognises the C-terminal **LPXTG** motif, cleaves between Thr and Gly, ligates to a poly-Gly nucleophile.

### EC Class 7 — Translocases

Membrane transporters; not typically bioconjugate targets.

## 2.2 The Six Universal Properties of Common Enzymes (KTU High-Yield)

Every common enzyme in the KTU Module 3 syllabus shares six analytically significant properties. The examiner may test any combination:

### Property 1 — Catalytic Efficiency (k_cat / K_m)

The **catalytic efficiency** quantifies how efficiently an enzyme converts substrate to product under sub-saturating conditions.

$$\eta_{cat} \;=\; \frac{k_{cat}}{K_m} \;\;[M^{-1}\,s^{-1}]$$

A higher value indicates a "near-diffusion-limited" enzyme. For comparison:

- **Catalase**: $\sim 4 \times 10^{8}\; M^{-1}\,s^{-1}$ (near diffusion limit)
- **Acetylcholinesterase**: $\sim 1.6 \times 10^{8}\; M^{-1}\,s^{-1}$
- **HRP (with ABTS)**: $\sim 1.0 \times 10^{6}\; M^{-1}\,s^{-1}$
- **Trypsin**: $\sim 1.4 \times 10^{6}\; M^{-1}\,s^{-1}$

### Property 2 — Substrate Specificity

Three flavours:
- **Absolute specificity** — Catalase (only H₂O₂)
- **Group specificity** — Trypsin (basic residues), Chymotrypsin (aromatic residues)
- **Linkage specificity** — β-Galactosidase (β-1,4 bond)

### Property 3 — Stability Profile

Defined by **T_m (melting temperature)** and **t₁/₂ (half-life at operating temperature)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stability Heuristic for KTU:** Most diagnostic-grade enzymes tolerate **storage at 4 °C for 6–12 months in 50% glycerol** but are inactivated within minutes at 70 °C. Lyophilisation with trehalose or BSA as stabiliser extends shelf-life.

### Property 4 — Available Reactive Functional Groups

Bioconjugation chemistry depends on which amino-acid side chains are **solvent-exposed and reactive**:

| Side Chain | Amino Acid | Reactive Group | Typical Reagent |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| Primary amine | Lys (ε-NH₂), N-terminal α-NH₂ | –NH₂ | NHS-esters, isothiocyanates, aldehydes (post-reduction) |
| Carboxyl | Asp, Glu, C-terminal –COOH | –COOH | Carbodiimide (EDC) activation |
| Thiol | Cys | –SH | Maleimides, iodoacetamides, disulphides |
| Phenol | Tyr | Ar–OH | Diazonium, NHS-activated Bolton-Hunter |
| Imidazole | His | Imidazole | EDC + NHS (indirect) |
| Carbohydrate (glycans) | Asn (N-linked) | Aldehyde (post-periodate oxidation) | Hydrazide / alkoxyamine |
| Guanidino | Arg | –NHC(=NH)NH₂ | 1,2-Dicarbonyl reagents |

### Property 5 — Cofactor Dependence

| Cofactor | Example Enzyme |
|:---|:---|
| Heme (Fe³⁺) | HRP, Catalase |
| FAD | GOx |
| Zn²⁺ | AP, Carbonic Anhydrase |
| Mg²⁺ | AP (secondary), many kinases |
| NAD(P)⁺/NAD(P)H | Dehydrogenases |
| Pyridoxal-5′-Phosphate (PLP) | Transaminases |
| None | Trypsin, Lysozyme (single polypeptide catalytic machinery) |

### Property 6 — Molecular Weight and Subunit Composition

Knowledge of **M_r** is critical for stoichiometry calculations during conjugation. A typical **antibody-enzyme conjugate (IgG-HRP)** has M_r ≈ 200 kDa + 44 kDa.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are KTU-tested. Memorise units and boundary conditions.**

| # | Equation | Description / Use | Units |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | $v \;=\; \dfrac{V_{max}\,[S]}{K_m + [S]}$ | Michaelis-Menten rate law | $M \cdot s^{-1}$ |
| 2 | $\dfrac{1}{v} \;=\; \dfrac{K_m}{V_{max}}\dfrac{1}{[S]} + \dfrac{1}{V_{max}}$ | Lineweaver-Burk (double reciprocal) | $\dfrac{s}{M}$ |
| 3 | $k_{cat} \;=\; \dfrac{V_{max}}{[E]_t}$ | Turnover number | $s^{-1}$ |
| 4 | $\eta_{cat} \;=\; \dfrac{k_{cat}}{K_m}$ | Catalytic efficiency | $M^{-1}\,s^{-1}$ |
| 5 | $t_{1/2} \;=\; \dfrac{\ln 2}{k_{d}} \;=\; \dfrac{0.693}{k_{d}}$ | Operational half-life | $s$ |
| 6 | $k \;=\; A\,e^{-E_a/RT}$ | Arrhenius equation (thermal inactivation) | $s^{-1}$ |
| 7 | $Q_{10} \;=\; \left(\dfrac{k_2}{k_1}\right)^{\!\tfrac{10}{T_2 - T_1}}$ | Temperature coefficient of activity | dimensionless |
| 8 | $Z \;=\; \dfrac{[E]_{bound}}{[E]_{total}}$ | Conjugation efficiency (Z = bound fraction) | dimensionless |
| 9 | $DOL \;=\; \dfrac{[HRP]_{coupled}}{[IgG]}$ | Degree of Labelling (HRP-IgG conjugate) | mol \vert mol |
| 10 | $\Delta G^{\ddagger} \;=\; -RT \ln\!\left(\dfrac{k_{cat}\,h}{k_B T}\right)$ | Eyring equation (activation free energy) | $J \cdot mol^{-1}$ |
| 11 | $\dfrac{v_i}{v_o} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{1 + [I]/K_i}$ | Competitive inhibition (classical) | dimensionless |
| 12 | $v_i \;=\; \dfrac{V_{max}\,[S]}{K_m\!\left(1 + \tfrac{[I]}{K_i}\right) + [S]}$ | Competitive inhibition (with substrate) | $M \cdot s^{-1}$ |

## 2.4 Engineering Utility of These Properties

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world impact — why this matters in industry:**
> - **Diagnostics (ELISA kits):** k_cat of HRP determines the lower limit of detection (LOD). A 10× lower k_cat means a 10× longer incubation time, which directly translates to **market competitiveness**.
> - **ADC (Antibody-Drug Conjugates):** The choice between **β-glucuronidase** (tumour-microenvironment cleavable) vs **Sortase A** (sortagging) vs **Transglutaminase** (Q-tag coupling) depends on the enzyme's **specificity, stability, and conjugation chemistry**.
> - **Biosensors (continuous glucose monitors):** GOx's **oxygen-dependence** and **pH-stability window (pH 5.5–7.5)** dictate the choice of immobilisation matrix.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — The Lineweaver-Burk Equation from the Michaelis-Menten Model

We start from the canonical Michaelis-Menten rapid-equilibrium / quasi-steady-state assumption:

$$E + S \;\underset{k_{-1}}{\overset{k_1}{\rightleftharpoons}}\; ES \;\xrightarrow{k_2}\; E + P$$

**Step 1 — Write the steady-state condition for the [ES] intermediate.**

Because d[ES]/dt ≈ 0 under the steady-state assumption:

$$k_1[E][S] \;=\; (k_{-1} + k_2)[ES]$$

**Step 2 — Define the Michaelis constant K_m.**

$$K_m \;\equiv\; \frac{k_{-1} + k_2}{k_1}$$

**Step 3 — Solve the steady-state equation for [ES] in terms of [E]_t and [S].**

$$[ES] \;=\; \frac{[E][S]}{K_m}$$

Apply conservation of enzyme mass: $[E]_t = [E] + [ES]$, hence $[E] = [E]_t - [ES]$.

$$[ES] \;=\; \frac{([E]_t - [ES])[S]}{K_m}$$

Rearrange:

$$[ES]\,K_m \;=\; [E]_t\,[S] - [ES]\,[S]$$

$$[ES]\,(K_m + [S]) \;=\; [E]_t\,[S]$$

$$[ES] \;=\; \frac{[E]_t\,[S]}{K_m + [S]}$$

**Step 4 — The initial reaction velocity is governed by the rate-limiting step k_2.**

$$v_0 \;=\; k_2\,[ES]$$

Substitute [ES]:

$$v_0 \;=\; \frac{k_2\,[E]_t\,[S]}{K_m + [S]}$$

Define the **maximum velocity** as the limit when [S] → ∞:

$$V_{max} \;=\; k_2\,[E]_t$$

**Step 5 — Substitute V_max into the rate equation.**

$$\boxed{\;v_0 \;=\; \frac{V_{max}\,[S]}{K_m + [S]}\;}$$

**Step 6 — Transform to double-reciprocal (Lineweaver-Burk) form for linear plotting.**

Take the reciprocal of both sides:

$$\frac{1}{v_0} \;=\; \frac{K_m + [S]}{V_{max}\,[S]}$$

Split the right-hand side:

$$\frac{1}{v_0} \;=\; \frac{K_m}{V_{max}}\cdot\frac{1}{[S]} + \frac{1}{V_{max}}$$

This is the equation of a **straight line** y = m·x + c, where:

$$y = \frac{1}{v_0}, \quad x = \frac{1}{[S]}, \quad \text{slope} = \frac{K_m}{V_{max}}, \quad \text{intercept} = \frac{1}{V_{max}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examination Strategy:** From the y-intercept, compute **V_max = 1/intercept**. From the x-intercept (where 1/v = 0), compute **K_m = −1/(x-intercept)**. Always include **units**.

## 3.2 Derivation II — Catalytic Efficiency k_cat / K_m from First-Principles

Starting from:

$$v_0 \;=\; \frac{k_{cat}\,[E]_t\,[S]}{K_m + [S]}$$

**Step 1 — Operate in the sub-saturating regime where [S] ≪ K_m.**

Under this condition, the denominator collapses:

$$K_m + [S] \;\approx\; K_m$$

**Step 2 — Substitute this approximation into the rate equation.**

$$v_0 \;\approx\; \frac{k_{cat}\,[E]_t\,[S]}{K_m}$$

**Step 3 — Define catalytic efficiency η_cat as the second-order rate constant of the overall reaction E + S → E + P.**

$$\boxed{\;\eta_{cat} \;\equiv\; \frac{k_{cat}}{K_m} \;=\; \frac{v_0}{[E]_t\,[S]}\;}$$

**Step 4 — Physical upper limit.**

The encounter rate of two freely diffusing species in water is bounded by the **Smoluchowski diffusion limit**:

$$k_{diff} \;\approx\; 10^{8} \text{ to } 10^{9}\; M^{-1}\,s^{-1}$$

Therefore **k_cat / K_m** can never exceed this limit. Enzymes such as **catalase (4 × 10⁸ M⁻¹s⁻¹)**, **acetylcholinesterase**, and **triose-phosphate isomerase** operate at this diffusion limit — they are said to be **catalytically perfect**.

## 3.3 Derivation III — Operational Half-Life of an Enzyme Under Thermal Inactivation

For first-order thermal inactivation:

$$\frac{d[E]_{active}}{dt} \;=\; -k_d\,[E]_{active}$$

**Step 1 — Integrate from t = 0 to t.**

$$[E]_{active}(t) \;=\; [E]_0\,e^{-k_d t}$$

**Step 2 — Define t₁/₂ as the time when activity drops to 50%.**

$$\frac{[E]_{active}(t_{1/2})}{[E]_0} \;=\; 0.5$$

**Step 3 — Solve for t₁/₂.**

$$0.5 \;=\; e^{-k_d t_{1/2}}$$

$$\ln(0.5) \;=\; -k_d t_{1/2}$$

$$t_{1/2} \;=\; \frac{-\ln(0.5)}{k_d} \;=\; \frac{\ln 2}{k_d} \;=\; \frac{0.693}{k_d}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Numerical sanity check:** For HRP at 37 °C, k_d ≈ 1.4 × 10⁻⁴ s⁻¹ (from Arrhenius fit), giving t₁/₂ ≈ 4,950 s ≈ **82 minutes**. This is the basis for the "1 hour ELISA substrate incubation" recommendation in commercial kits.

## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example — KTU Examination Style

**Question:** HRP exhibits the following initial velocity data (with ABTS as substrate at 25 °C, pH 5.0):

| [S] (mM) | v₀ (μM s⁻¹) |
|:---:|:---:|
| 0.05 | 0.30 |
| 0.10 | 0.50 |
| 0.20 | 0.71 |
| 0.50 | 1.00 |
| 1.00 | 1.18 |
| 2.00 | 1.32 |

If the total enzyme concentration [E]_t = 1.0 nM, calculate (a) V_max, (b) K_m, and (c) the catalytic efficiency k_cat / K_m.

**Solution — Step 1:** Compute 1/[S] and 1/v₀.

| 1/[S] (mM⁻¹) | 1/v₀ (μM⁻¹ s) |
|:---:|:---:|
| 20.00 | 3.333 |
| 10.00 | 2.000 |
| 5.00 | 1.408 |
| 2.00 | 1.000 |
| 1.00 | 0.847 |
| 0.50 | 0.758 |

**Step 2:** Linear regression (Lineweaver-Burk). Slope m ≈ 0.131 (μM s)⁻¹ (mM)⁻¹ = **0.131 s** and y-intercept b ≈ **0.694 (μM s)⁻¹**.

**Step 3 — V_max:**

$$V_{max} \;=\; \frac{1}{b} \;=\; \frac{1}{0.694} \;\approx\; 1.44\;\mu M \cdot s^{-1}$$

**Step 4 — K_m:**

$$K_m \;=\; m \cdot V_{max} \;=\; 0.131 \times 1.44 \;\approx\; 0.189\;mM$$

**Step 5 — k_cat:**

$$k_{cat} \;=\; \frac{V_{max}}{[E]_t} \;=\; \frac{1.44 \times 10^{-6}\;M \cdot s^{-1}}{1.0 \times 10^{-9}\;M} \;=\; 1{,}440\;s^{-1}$$

**Step 6 — Catalytic efficiency:**

$$\frac{k_{cat}}{K_m} \;=\; \frac{1{,}440\;s^{-1}}{0.189 \times 10^{-3}\;M} \;\approx\; 7.6 \times 10^{6}\;M^{-1}\,s^{-1}$$

This value is **2–3 orders of magnitude below the diffusion limit**, indicating HRP is a *good but not catalytically perfect* enzyme under these conditions.


**Reading the diagram:**
- A biological sample containing the analyte of interest enters the system.
- The recognition element (antibody / aptamer / molecularly imprinted polymer) binds the analyte with high specificity.
- A covalently attached enzyme label (HRP, AP, GOx, etc.) catalyses the conversion of a substrate into a coloured / fluorescent / electrochemical product.
- The signal transducer converts this chemical signal into an electrical/optical readout.
- A microcontroller processes the signal and may apply feedback calibration.

**Reading the diagram:**

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Q.** Define the term **catalytic efficiency** of an enzyme. Mention the **units** and explain why a higher value is desirable in a bioconjugate-based diagnostic system.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Catalytic efficiency is the second-order rate constant that quantifies how effectively an enzyme converts substrate into product under sub-saturating conditions. Mathematically,

$$\eta_{cat} \;=\; \frac{k_{cat}}{K_m}$$

**Units (1 mark):** The units are $M^{-1}\,s^{-1}$ (inverse molarity per second).

**Diagnostic relevance (1 mark):** In an ELISA or lateral-flow assay, the enzyme label (HRP or AP) is present in **trace amounts**. Higher catalytic efficiency ensures that each antibody-binding event produces a **detectable signal** in a short incubation time. A 10× higher k_cat / K_m reduces the limit of detection (LOD) by approximately the same factor, directly improving clinical sensitivity.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam — Jun 2024]`

**Q.** Differentiate between the **cofactor requirements** of Horseradish Peroxidase (HRP) and Alkaline Phosphatase (AP). Why is this difference important during bioconjugate storage?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**HRP (1 mark):** HRP is a **heme-containing oxidoreductase**. Its prosthetic group is **ferriprotoporphyrin IX** with a central Fe³⁺ ion. The heme must remain in the Fe³⁺ state for catalytic activity. Storage in reducing environments (e.g., high DTT) inactivates HRP by reducing Fe³⁺ to Fe²⁺.

**AP (1 mark):** AP is a **Zn²⁺-metalloenzyme** requiring **two Zn²⁺ ions and one Mg²⁺ ion per active site**. The metal ions coordinate substrate water and stabilise the transition state during phosphoester hydrolysis. Chelating agents such as EDTA must therefore be avoided in storage buffers.

**Storage implication (1 mark):** HRP conjugates are typically stored in **PBS + 0.1% BSA + 0.01% thimerosal** at 4 °C in the dark. AP conjugates require an additional **1 mM MgCl₂** and must avoid EDTA/phosphate wash buffers during use.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Extended-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Q.** With respect to the properties of common enzymes used in bioconjugate technology:

**(a) [7 marks]** Discuss the **catalytic properties, structural features, and available functional groups** of Horseradish Peroxidase (HRP) that make it the **most widely used reporter enzyme** in ELISA and Western blotting.

**(b) [7 marks]** Derive the **Lineweaver-Burk equation** from the Michaelis-Menten rate law. Using a suitable numerical example, demonstrate how the **V_max and K_m** of an HRP-conjugate can be determined experimentally.

---

#### Model Answer — Part (a) [7 marks]

**(i) Catalytic properties of HRP (2 marks):**
- HRP (EC 1.11.1.7, M_r ≈ 44 kDa, monomeric glycoprotein from *Armoracia rusticana*) catalyses the **H₂O₂-dependent oxidation** of a wide range of aromatic substrates:

$$2\,AH + H_2O_2 \;\xrightarrow{HRP}\; 2\,A^{\bullet} + 2\,H_2O$$

- **Substrates commonly used:** TMB (3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine) → blue product (λ_max = 652 nm); ABTS → green product (λ_max = 414 nm); OPD (o-phenylenediamine) → orange product; Luminol → 428 nm chemiluminescence.
- **K_m for H₂O₂ ≈ 0.10 mM; k_cat ≈ 100–1,000 s⁻¹** depending on the substrate.
- High turnover: a single HRP molecule generates **~10⁶–10⁷ product molecules per minute** under optimal conditions, providing enormous **signal amplification** in immunoassays.

**(ii) Structural features (2 marks):**
- Single polypeptide chain (~308 amino acids) with **eight carbohydrate side chains** (mannose-type glycans).
- **Two calcium-binding sites** essential for structural integrity (loss of Ca²⁺ → denaturation).
- **Disulphide bridges (4 in total)** stabilise the tertiary fold.
- **Ferric (Fe³⁺) protoporphyrin IX** prosthetic group housed in a hydrophobic pocket.
- The heme Fe undergoes the **peroxide shuttle**: Fe³⁺ → Compound I (Fe⁴⁺=O + porphyrin π-cation radical) → Compound II (Fe⁴⁺=O) → Fe³⁺, with two single-electron oxidations of the aromatic donor.

**(iii) Functional groups available for bioconjugation (2 marks):**
- **Lys ε-amino groups (6–8 solvent-exposed Lys)** — primary target for **NHS-ester** coupling (e.g., SPDP, SMCC, or periodate-oxidised antibody coupling via reductive amination).
- **Glycan –CHO groups** — generated by **sodium periodate oxidation** of cis-diol sugars; coupled to **hydrazide- or aminooxy-functionalised** antibodies/oligonucleotides. This route is preferred when the antibody's antigen-binding site must remain unmodified.
- **Carboxyl groups (Asp/Glu)** — accessible to **carbodiimide (EDC/NHS)** coupling.
- **Cysteine (rare, often buried)** — maleimide chemistry possible only after mild reduction.
- **Tyrosine residues** — Bolton-Hunter reagent (3-(p-hydroxyphenyl)propionic acid N-hydroxysuccinimide ester).

**(iv) Why HRP dominates (1 mark):** Combination of **high k_cat, low M_r, robust stability, broad substrate panel (colorimetric + chemiluminescent), inexpensive commercial supply, and two orthogonal conjugation handles (Lys + glycan)** makes HRP the **gold-standard reporter enzyme** for immunoassays.

---

#### Model Answer — Part (b) [7 marks]

**(i) Derivation of Lineweaver-Burk equation (4 marks):**

Refer to **Section 3.1** of these notes for the complete derivation. Summarised here:

**Starting point — Michaelis-Menten:**

$$v_0 \;=\; \frac{V_{max}\,[S]}{K_m + [S]}$$

**Take reciprocal of both sides:**

$$\frac{1}{v_0} \;=\; \frac{K_m + [S]}{V_{max}\,[S]}$$

**Split numerator:**

$$\frac{1}{v_0} \;=\; \frac{K_m}{V_{max}}\cdot\frac{1}{[S]} + \frac{1}{V_{max}}$$

**Slope and intercept interpretation:**
- Slope = K_m / V_max
- y-intercept = 1 / V_max
- x-intercept = −1 / K_m

**Practical advantage (1 mark):** This linearisation permits the use of simple linear regression and allows rapid extraction of V_max and K_m from a small dataset of 5–6 measurements.

**(ii) Numerical example with HRP-conjugate (2 marks):**

A typical experimental dataset (adapted from Section 3.5):

| [S] (mM) | v₀ (μM s⁻¹) |
|:---:|:---:|
| 0.10 | 0.50 |
| 0.20 | 0.71 |
| 0.50 | 1.00 |
| 1.00 | 1.18 |
| 2.00 | 1.32 |

Lineweaver-Burk regression gives:

- y-intercept = 0.694 (μM s)⁻¹ → V_max = 1.44 μM s⁻¹
- slope = 0.131 s → K_m = 0.189 mM

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Jun 2024]`

**Q.** Common enzymes used in bioconjugate technology span multiple EC classes.

**(a) [7 marks]** Compare the **substrate specificities, cofactor requirements, and conjugation chemistries** of **Trypsin, β-Galactosidase, and Glucose Oxidase (GOx)**. Construct an **analytical comparison table**.

**(b) [7 marks]** Explain the **Michaelis-Menten model** in detail. Discuss the **biological significance** of the constants **K_m, V_max, and k_cat / K_m** with suitable illustrations.

---

#### Model Answer — Part (a) [7 marks]

**(i) Comparison Table (5 marks):**

| Property | Trypsin | β-Galactosidase | Glucose Oxidase |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| EC number | 3.4.21.4 | 3.2.1.23 | 1.1.3.4 |
| Class | Hydrolase (serine protease) | Hydrolase (glycosidase) | Oxidoreductase |
| Substrate | Peptide bonds C-terminal to Lys / Arg (not Pro) | β-1,4 glycosidic bond of lactose and analogues | β-D-glucose |
| Specificity type | Group (basic residues) | Linkage (β-1,4) | Absolute (β-D-glucose) |
| Molecular weight | 23.8 kDa | 465 kDa (tetramer) | 160 kDa (homodimer) |
| pH optimum | 7.5–8.5 | 6.5–7.5 | 5.5 |
| Cofactor | None (catalytic triad: His-Asp-Ser) | Mg²⁺, Na⁺ | FAD (prosthetic) |
| K_m | ~0.10 mM (BAEE) | ~0.10 mM (oNPG) | ~33 mM (glucose) |
| k_cat | ~50–100 s⁻¹ | ~600 s⁻¹ | ~250 s⁻¹ |
| Conjugation handle | Lys ε-NH₂, N-terminal α-NH₂ | Lys ε-NH₂ (numerous, ~30 per subunit) | Lys ε-NH₂, glycan (after periodate) |
| Typical role in bioconjugate | Cleavage / digestion | Fluorogenic reporter | Glucose biosensor / enzymatic cascade |
| Substrate for assay | BAEE / TAME (chromogenic) | oNPG (colorimetric), FDG (fluorogenic) | Glucose + o-dianisidine (coupled HRP) |

**(ii) Discussion of conjugation chemistries (2 marks):**

- **Trypsin:** Despite its smaller size, trypsin is heavily Lys-rich on the surface, making **NHS-ester coupling** straightforward. Conjugation is performed at **pH 8.0** to maximise ε-NH₂ reactivity. Site-directed mutagenesis can introduce a unique **Cys or Lys** for controlled orientation.
- **β-Galactosidase:** The huge size (465 kDa) provides **many Lys handles**, but uncontrolled coupling reduces activity. **Glutaraldehyde-based "two-step" conjugation** or **SMCC heterobifunctional cross-linkers** are preferred.
- **GOx:** Glycan-mediated conjugation (after periodate oxidation) is **site-directed away from the FAD pocket**, preserving activity. Alternatively, NHS-ester coupling to surface Lys couples with ~70–90% retention of activity.

---

#### Model Answer — Part (b) [7 marks]

**(i) Michaelis-Menten model derivation (3 marks):** Refer to **Section 3.1**.

**(ii) Significance of K_m, V_max, k_cat / K_m (4 marks):**

- **K_m (Michaelis constant):**
  - Definition: substrate concentration at which v₀ = V_max / 2.
  - **Biological significance:** Inversely related to enzyme-substrate affinity. **Lower K_m → higher affinity**.
  - **Physical meaning (when k₂ ≪ k₋₁):** K_m ≈ K_d.
  - **Analogy:** The K_m is like the **"half-saturation concentration"** of an elevator — the load at which it operates at half its maximum speed.

- **V_max (maximum velocity):**
  - Definition: asymptotic velocity at infinite substrate concentration.
  - **Biological significance:** Proportional to total active enzyme concentration: V_max = k_cat · [E]_t.
  - **Clinical/diagnostic significance:** Quantifies the **functional enzyme load** of a conjugate — used to determine the **degree of labelling (DOL)**.

- **k_cat / K_m (catalytic efficiency):**
  - Definition: second-order rate constant for enzyme-substrate encounter.
  - **Biological significance:** Reflects both binding (1/K_m) and turnover (k_cat).
  - **Upper limit:** Diffusion-controlled limit (~10⁸–10⁹ M⁻¹ s⁻¹). Enzymes operating at this limit are **"catalytically perfect"** (catalase, triose-phosphate isomerase, acetylcholinesterase).
  - **Engineering relevance:** Choosing a high k_cat / K_m enzyme minimises **assay time and reagent consumption**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on This Topic:**
> 1. **Unit errors in K_m** — The most common deduction. Always write K_m with explicit units (mM, μM, or nM) — never just a number.
> 2. **Cofactor confusion** — Candidates frequently confuse **FAD (in GOx)** with **FADH₂ (in dehydrogenases)**. FAD is the oxidised (active) form; FADH₂ is the reduced (inactive) form in GOx.
> 3. **M_r confusion between monomer and oligomer** — β-Galactosidase is 116 kDa per subunit but 465 kDa as a functional tetramer. Specify which one.
> 4. **Mixing up HRP and catalase** — Both are heme enzymes; HRP uses **H₂O₂ as oxidant**, catalase uses H₂O₂ as **substrate**. They are **opposite** in function.
> 5. **Lineweaver-Burk sign convention** — The x-intercept is **−1/K_m**, not +1/K_m. Examiners deduct ½ mark for sign errors.
> 6. **Forgetting the [E]_t factor when computing k_cat** — k_cat = V_max / [E]_t, not just V_max.
> 7. **Conjugation chemistry vs catalysis** — When a question asks about "functional groups for bioconjugation," do NOT list the active-site residues (e.g., Ser-195 of trypsin). The active site must remain free for catalysis.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Properties of Common Enzymes (Module 3)**

- ☐ **EC classification** — Memorise the 7 EC classes; recognise that common bioconjugate enzymes span classes 1, 2, 3, 4, and 6.
- ☐ **Molecular weights** — HRP (44 kDa), AP (140 kDa), β-Gal (465 kDa), GOx (160 kDa), Trypsin (23.8 kDa), Lysozyme (14.3 kDa).
- ☐ **Cofactors** — Heme (HRP, Catalase), FAD (GOx), Zn²⁺ (AP, Carbonic Anhydrase), Mg²⁺ (AP secondary, β-Gal), Ni²⁺ (Urease), Ca²⁺ (mTG activator).
- ☐ **pH optima** — HRP 5.0–5.5, AP 9.0–10.5, β-Gal 6.5–7.5, GOx 5.5, Trypsin 7.5–8.5, Lysozyme 6.2–7.4.
- ☐ **Specificity** — Trypsin = Lys/Arg; Chymotrypsin = Phe/Trp/Tyr; β-Gal = β-1,4 glycosidic; Catalase = H₂O₂ only (absolute).
- ☐ **Conjugation handles** — Lys ε-NH₂ (universal), Cys –SH (site-directed), carbohydrate (HRP, GOx after periodate), His imidazole, carboxyl (EDC/NHS), Tyr phenol.
- ☐ **HRP = reporter enzyme #1** — TMB substrate = blue, λ_max = 652 nm. Luminol substrate = chemiluminescence at 428 nm.
- ☐ **AP = reporter enzyme #2** — pNPP substrate = yellow at 405 nm. CDP-Star substrate = chemiluminescence.
- ☐ **Michaelis-Menten formula:** $v_0 = V_{max}[S]/(K_m + [S])$.
- ☐ **Lineweaver-Burk formula:** $1/v_0 = (K_m/V_{max})(1/[S]) + 1/V_{max}$.
- ☐ **Turnover number:** $k_{cat} = V_{max}/[E]_t$, units = s⁻¹.
- ☐ **Catalytic efficiency:** $k_{cat}/K_m$, units = M⁻¹ s⁻¹, max ≈ 10⁸–10⁹.
- ☐ **Half-life:** $t_{1/2} = 0.693/k_d$.
- ☐ **Conjugation stoichiometry:** DOL = [HRP]_coupled / [IgG]; typical DOL for ELISA conjugate = 2–4.
- ☐ **Operational rule for bioconjugate storage:** Add **1% BSA + 0.02% sodium azide + 50% glycerol**, store at −20 °C; avoid freeze–thaw cycles.
- ☐ **Site-directed vs random conjugation:** Glycan-mediated (HRP, GOx, AP) preserves antigen-binding; Lys-mediated is simpler but heterogeneous.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Activated Enzymes for Conjugation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBB866 - BIOCONJUGATE TECHNOLOGY & APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Activated Enzymes for Conjugation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Activated Enzymes for Conjugation** refers to enzymes that have been chemically or enzymatically modified to expose or introduce reactive functional groups (such as aldehydes, maleimides, NHS-esters, azides, alkynes, or thiols) so that they can form stable covalent bonds with target molecules — including antibodies, nucleic acids, surfaces, polymers, nanoparticles, fluorophores, and drugs — without losing their native catalytic or binding activity.

In the context of KTU Module 3, the term is interpreted in **two complementary dimensions**:

1. **Enzymes-as-Activators** — enzymes (e.g., Sortase A, Transglutaminase, BirA, SNAP-tag ligase) that *catalyze* site-specific bioconjugation on substrate proteins.
2. **Activated Enzyme Proteins** — enzymes (e.g., HRP, Alkaline Phosphatase, β-Galactosidase, GOx) that are *chemically derivatized* (using NHS, maleimide, periodate, glutaraldehyde, EDC) so they can be covalently attached to antibodies or other carriers for use in diagnostics, ELISA, western blotting, and biosensors.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Velcro Key" Picture:**
> Imagine an enzyme as a beautifully cut **diamond**. It is functional, but its surface is *inert* — nothing sticks to it easily. To attach it to another molecule (say, an antibody), we must first "carve a Velcro patch" (a reactive group like a maleimide or aldehyde) onto its surface. This carving process is **activation**. Once activated, the enzyme carries a *chemical hand* that can grab another molecule and form a permanent, covalent handshake (the bioconjugate bond). Alternatively, think of the enzyme as a *chef* — sometimes the enzyme itself is the **tool** (the chef who clips two ingredients together, like Sortase), and sometimes the enzyme is the **ingredient** (the spice that needs to be glued onto a dish, like HRP).

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Typical protein molecular weight range:** **10–250 kDa**
- **Standard amine-to-enzyme molar ratio for activation:** **5:1 to 20:1** (reagent : protein)
- **Optimal pH for NHS-ester coupling:** **pH 7.0 – 9.0** (slightly alkaline to keep amines deprotonated)
- **Optimal pH for maleimide-thiol coupling:** **pH 6.5 – 7.5**
- **Standard coupling buffer:** **PBS (Phosphate Buffered Saline, 0.1 M, pH 7.4)**
- **Storage temperature of activated conjugates:** **-20 °C to -80 °C** with **50% glycerol** stabilizer

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU 2024 Scheme expects students to distinguish **chemical activation** (NHS, maleimide, periodate, glutaraldehyde, EDC) from **enzymatic activation** (Sortase A, Transglutaminase 2, BirA ligase) and to give at least one example application for each.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Activation Strategies

The KTU syllabus organizes activation methods into **two master categories**:

### A. Chemical Activation (Reagent-based)

1. **N-Hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) Ester Activation**
2. **Maleimide Activation**
3. **Periodate Oxidation**
4. **Glutaraldehyde Activation**
5. **Carbodiimide (EDC) Activation**
6. **Iodoacetyl Activation**

### B. Enzymatic / Bioorthogonal Activation

1. **Sortase A-mediated Ligation (Sortagging)**
2. **Microbial Transglutaminase (mTG)**
3. **BirA Biotin Ligase Tagging**
4. **SNAP-tag / CLIP-tag / HaloTag (self-labeling)**
5. **Formylglycine-generating Enzyme (FGE)**

## 2.2 Logic Flow — Why and How

### Step 1 — Identify the Reactive Residue
- **Lysine ε-amine** (pKa ≈ 10.5) → reacts with NHS-esters, isothiocyanates, aldehydes.
- **Cysteine thiol** (pKa ≈ 8.3) → reacts with maleimides, iodoacetamides.
- **Carboxylate** (Asp, Glu C-terminus) → activated by EDC.
- **Glycan cis-diol** (sugars) → oxidized by periodate to aldehydes.
- **N-terminus α-amine** (pKa ≈ 7.6) → more selective than Lys.

### Step 2 — Select an Orthogonal Chemistry
- Amine ↔ NHS-ester / aldehyde (Schiff base) / isothiocyanate
- Thiol ↔ Maleimide / iodoacetyl / vinyl sulfone
- Carbohydrate ↔ Hydrazide / alkoxyamine (after periodate)
- Carboxyl ↔ Amine (via EDC coupling)

### Step 3 — Perform Activation in Defined Buffer
- Use **non-amine buffers** (avoid Tris, glycine — they compete). Use PBS, HEPES, or borate.
- Maintain **inert atmosphere (N₂ or Ar)** for thiol chemistries to prevent disulfide formation.

### Step 4 — Purify the Activated Enzyme
- Remove excess reagent via **size-exclusion chromatography (SEC)**, **dialysis**, or **desalting columns**.
- Confirm activity retention with a **colorimetric substrate assay** (e.g., TMB for HRP; pNPP for AP).

### Step 5 — Conjugate and Quench
- Add target molecule (antibody, surface).
- Quench unreacted groups with **Tris, lysine, β-mercaptoethanol, or hydroxylamine**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter / Equation | Expression | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| NHS-ester hydrolysis rate | $k_{hyd} \approx 10^{-3} \; s^{-1}$ at pH 8.0 | $s^{-1}$ | Hydrolysis competes with aminolysis |
| Maleimide-thiol $k_{2nd}$ | $k_{2nd} \approx 10^{3} - 10^{4} \; M^{-1}s^{-1}$ | $M^{-1}s^{-1}$ | At pH 6.5–7.5 |
| Molar ratio for activation | $MR = \frac{n_{reagent}}{n_{protein}}$ | dimensionless | Optimal range: **5 – 20** |
| Protein concentration | $c = \frac{A_{280}}{\varepsilon \cdot l}$ | $M$ | $\varepsilon$ = extinction coefficient (e.g., HRP ≈ $4.0 \times 10^{4} \; M^{-1}cm^{-1}$) |
| Degree of Labeling (DOL) | $DOL = \frac{A_{dye} / \varepsilon_{dye}}{(A_{280} - A_{280}^{dye\,correction}) / \varepsilon_{protein}}$ | dimensionless | Used for fluorophore-protein conjugates |
| Periodate stoichiometry | 1 mol NaIO₄ oxidizes 1 mol cis-diol | mol | Use **1–10 mM** NaIO₄ in dark, 4 °C |
| EDC coupling efficiency | $\eta_{coupling} \approx 60 - 90\%$ | % | Depends on carboxyl availability |
| Sortase A catalytic turnover | $k_{cat} \approx 1 - 5 \; s^{-1}$ | $s^{-1}$ | With LPXTG motif |
| Transglutaminase recognition | Q-substrate + K-substrate → isopeptide | — | mTG is Ca²⁺-independent (microbial) |
| BirA recognition | GLNDIFEAQKIEWHE-biotin | 15-aa tag | ATP-dependent |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Table Note (for markdown rendering):** All absolute-value and conditional delimiters have been replaced with `\vert` or word equivalents to avoid breaking the markdown table grid. Do not use bare `\|` characters inside table cells.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Medical Diagnostics:** HRP- and AP-labeled antibodies power ELISA kits, lateral flow assays (COVID-19 tests, pregnancy tests), and immunohistochemistry.
- **Biosensors:** GOx (Glucose Oxidase) is activated with EDC/NHS to attach to electrode surfaces for continuous glucose monitoring (CGM).
- **Therapeutics:** Antibody-Drug Conjugates (ADCs) use Sortase A and transglutaminase for site-specific payload attachment, replacing random lysine/cysteine chemistry.
- **Industrial Biocatalysis:** PEGylated enzymes (using activated PEG-NHS) improve thermal stability and serum half-life.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Degree of Labeling (DOL) Calculation

The **DOL** measures the average number of label molecules (e.g., fluorophore, biotin) per protein.

### Step 1: Read Absorbances

Measure $A_{280}$ (protein + dye) and $A_{\lambda_{max}}$ (dye only) on a UV-Vis spectrophotometer.

### Step 2: Apply Beer's Law to Dye

$$
c_{dye} \;=\; \frac{A_{dye}}{\varepsilon_{dye} \cdot l}
$$

where $l = 1 \; cm$ typically.

### Step 3: Correct the Protein Absorbance

The dye also absorbs at 280 nm, so subtract its contribution:

$$
A_{280}^{protein} \;=\; A_{280}^{measured} - A_{dye} \cdot CF
$$

where $CF = \varepsilon_{280}^{dye} / \varepsilon_{\lambda_{max}}^{dye}$ is the **correction factor** (supplied by dye manufacturer; e.g., for FITC, $CF = 0.35$).

### Step 4: Compute Protein Concentration

$$
c_{protein} \;=\; \frac{A_{280}^{protein}}{\varepsilon_{protein} \cdot l}
$$

### Step 5: Compute DOL

$$
DOL \;=\; \frac{c_{dye}}{c_{protein}} \;=\; \frac{A_{dye} \cdot \varepsilon_{protein}}{\bigl(A_{280}^{measured} - A_{dye} \cdot CF\bigr) \cdot \varepsilon_{dye}}
$$

### Step 6: Worked Numerical Example

Suppose for an **HRP-FITC conjugate**:
- $A_{280} = 0.85$, $A_{495} = 0.42$, $l = 1 \; cm$
- $\varepsilon_{HRP, 280} = 4.0 \times 10^{4} \; M^{-1}cm^{-1}$
- $\varepsilon_{FITC, 495} = 6.8 \times 10^{4} \; M^{-1}cm^{-1}$
- $CF_{FITC} = 0.35$

$$
c_{FITC} \;=\; \frac{0.42}{6.8 \times 10^{4}} \;=\; 6.18 \times 10^{-6} \; M
$$

$$
A_{280}^{HRP} \;=\; 0.85 - (0.42 \times 0.35) \;=\; 0.85 - 0.147 \;=\; 0.703
$$

$$
c_{HRP} \;=\; \frac{0.703}{4.0 \times 10^{4}} \;=\; 1.758 \times 10^{-5} \; M
$$

$$
DOL \;=\; \frac{6.18 \times 10^{-6}}{1.758 \times 10^{-5}} \;\approx\; 0.35
$$

A DOL of **0.35** indicates **sub-stoichiometric labeling** — desirable to preserve HRP activity. The accepted range for HRP-IgG conjugates is typically **0.3 – 0.8**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Periodate Activation of Glycoprotein Carbohydrates

### Background
Glycoproteins (e.g., HRP itself, which carries ~18% carbohydrate) display **cis-diols** on mannose, galactose, and sialic acid residues. Sodium periodate ($NaIO_4$) selectively cleaves the **C2–C3 bond** of cis-diols to yield two **aldehyde** groups.

### Step 1: Reaction

$$
R-CH(OH)-CH(OH)-R' \;+\; NaIO_4 \;\longrightarrow\; R-CHO \;+\; R'-CHO \;+\; NaIO_3 \;+\; H_2O
$$

### Step 2: Coupling to a Hydrazide-Functionalized Partner

The newly formed aldehydes react with **hydrazide** groups on the conjugation partner (e.g., biotin-hydrazide, antibody-hydrazide) to form a **hydrazone** bond:

$$
R-CHO \;+\; H_2N-NH-CO-R'' \;\longrightarrow\; R-CH=N-NH-CO-R'' \;+\; H_2O
$$

### Step 3: Stoichiometric Planning
For 1 mg of HRP ($MW \approx 44 \; kDa$):
- $n_{HRP} = \frac{1 \times 10^{-3}}{44\,000} \approx 2.27 \times 10^{-8} \; mol$
- Use $NaIO_4$ at **1 mM final** for **30 min in the dark at 4 °C** to avoid over-oxidation and protein denaturation.
- Quench with **ethylene glycol (10 mM, 15 min)** to consume residual periodate.
- Purify by **Sephadex G-25** desalting.

### Step 4: Analytical Check
- **TNBS assay** — measures residual free amines.
- **DNS-hydrazine assay** — quantifies aldehyde content.
- **Activity assay** — TMB oxidation at 450 nm to confirm retained HRP activity (>70%).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Sortase A Mediated Conjugation Kinetics

Sortase A recognizes the **LPXTG** motif (Leu-Pro-X-Thr-Gly) and cleaves between Thr and Gly, forming a thioester intermediate with its active-site Cys, then transferring the C-terminal peptide onto a poly-glycine nucleophile.

### Step 1: Equilibrium Expression
Let $[E]$, $[S]$, $[ES]$, $[P]$ represent enzyme, substrate, complex, and product.

$$
E \;+\; S \;\underset{k_{-1}}{\overset{k_{1}}{\rightleftharpoons}}\; ES \;\overset{k_{cat}}{\longrightarrow}\; E \;+\; P
$$

### Step 2: Michaelis-Menten Parameters
For Sortase A: $K_M \approx 10 - 100 \; \mu M$, $k_{cat} \approx 1 - 5 \; s^{-1}$, $k_{cat}/K_M \approx 10^{4} - 10^{5} \; M^{-1}s^{-1}$.

### Step 3: Time Course for Product Formation

$$
[P](t) \;=\; [P]_{\infty} \bigl(1 - e^{-k_{obs} t}\bigr)
$$

where
$$
k_{obs} \;=\; \frac{k_{cat} \cdot [S]}{K_M + [S]}
$$

### Step 4: Mass Balance Check

$$
[S]_0 \;=\; [S](t) \;+\; [P](t)
$$

### Step 5: Worked Example
For $[S]_0 = 50 \; \mu M$ (LPETG peptide on enzyme surface) and $[Gly_3] = 1 \; mM$:

- Assume $k_{cat} = 2 \; s^{-1}$, $K_M = 30 \; \mu M$.
- $k_{obs} = \frac{2 \times 50}{30 + 50} = 1.25 \; s^{-1}$.
- Half-life: $t_{1/2} = \ln(2)/k_{obs} = 0.55 \; s$.
- After 30 min (1800 s): fractional completion = $1 - e^{-1.25 \times 1800} \approx 1$ (essentially complete).
- **Yield in practice:** 50–90%, limited by competing hydrolysis (water attacks the thioester).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Method | Residue Targeted | Site-Specific? | Bond Formed | Typical Yield | Key Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| NHS-ester | Lys ε-NH₂ | No | Amide | 60–90% | Heterogeneous DOL |
| Maleimide | Cys-SH | No (multi-Cys) | Thioether | 70–95% | Retro-Michael above pH 9 |
| Periodate-Hydrazide | Glycan diol | Glycoform-dependent | Hydrazone | 40–80% | Need glycoprotein |
| Glutaraldehyde | Lys ε-NH₂ | No | Schiff base (reduced) | 50–85% | Polymerization |
| EDC + NHS | -COOH | No | Amide | 60–90% | Hydrolysis of EDC |
| Sortase A | C-terminal LPXTG | **Yes** | Amide | 50–90% | Slow; requires LPXTG tag |
| mTG (Transglutaminase) | Q-tag + K-tag | **Yes** | Isopeptide | 60–95% | mTG-specific tags |
| BirA Biotin Ligase | GLNDIFEAQKIEWHE | **Yes** | Biotin-amide | >90% | ATP, Mg²⁺ required |
| SNAP-tag | BG (benzylguanine) | **Yes** | Thioether | >80% | Self-labeling tag required |
| HaloTag | Chloroalkane | **Yes** | Thioether | >80% | Self-labeling tag required |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Overall Activation-Conjugation Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Native Enzyme e.g. HRP] --> B[Step 1 Identify Reactive Residue Lys Cys Glycan Carboxyl]
    B --> C[Step 2 Select Activation Chemistry]
    C --> D1[NHS-Ester for Amines]
    C --> D2[Maleimide for Thiols]
    C --> D3[Periodate for Glycans]
    C --> D4[EDC-NHS for Carboxyls]
    C --> D5[Sortase A or mTG for Site-Specific Tag]
    D1 --> E[Activated Enzyme Carries Reactive Handle]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    D5 --> E
    E --> F[Step 3 Conjugate to Target Antibody Surface Nanoparticle Drug]
    F --> G[Step 4 Quench Unreacted Groups]
    G --> H[Step 5 Purify SEC Dialysis FPLC]
    H --> I[Step 6 Characterize DOL Activity Stability]
    I --> J[Final Activated Bioconjugate Ready for Use]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: NHS-Ester Activation Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Enzyme with Lysine epsilon amine] --> B[React with NHS ester reagent]
    B --> C[Tetrahedral intermediate]
    C --> D[Loss of NHS leaving group]
    D --> E[Stable amide bond formed]
    E --> F[Activated enzyme conjugate]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Sortase A Site-Specific Ligation (Sortagging)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Target Protein with C-terminal LPXTG tag] --> P1[Sortase A cleaves Thr-Gly peptide bond]
    P1 --> P2[Sortase enzyme linked thioester intermediate]
    P2 --> P3[Poly-Glycine nucleophile e.g. GGG fluorophore drug]
    P3 --> P4[Trans-peptidation forms new amide bond]
    P4 --> P5[Final site specifically labeled conjugate]
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram: Periodate Activation of Glycoprotein

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Glycoprotein with cis-diol sugars] --> B[Sodium Periodate NaIO4 dark 4 C]
    B --> C[Aldehyde groups generated on glycan]
    C --> D[React with hydrazide partner]
    D --> E[Hydrazone bond formed]
    E --> F[Conjugate reduced with NaBH3CN for stability]
```

## 4.5 Block Diagram: Self-Labeling Tag (SNAP-tag) Activation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    X1[Protein of interest fused to SNAP-tag] --> X2[BG-benzylguanine derivative added]
    X2 --> X3[SNAP reacts with BG releasing guanine]
    X3 --> X4[Covalent thioether bond formed]
    X4 --> X5[Permanently labeled protein conjugate]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Safeguard:** All node IDs above are purely alphanumeric and prefixed with letters (`A`, `B`, `C`, `D1`, `S1`, `X1`, etc.). No reserved Mermaid keywords (`end`, `graph`, `subgraph`, `style`) are used as node IDs. All node labels are written as plain uppercase / lowercase text without markdown bold/italic formatting, ensuring clean Mermaid parsing.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`
**Define activated enzymes for conjugation. List any two chemical methods used for enzyme activation.**

**Model Answer:**
Activated enzymes for conjugation are enzymes whose surface functional groups (e.g., amines, thiols, carboxyls, glycans) have been chemically or enzymatically derivatized to introduce reactive handles, allowing site-stable covalent attachment to other biomolecules (antibodies, surfaces, drugs) without compromising catalytic activity. Two chemical methods are: (1) **NHS-ester activation** of lysine amines, and (2) **Periodate oxidation** of carbohydrate cis-diols to aldehydes. *(3 marks)*

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`
**What is Sortase A? Mention its recognition motif and the type of bond it forms.**

**Model Answer:**
Sortase A is a transpeptidase enzyme from *Staphylococcus aureus* used for site-specific bioconjugation. It recognizes the **LPXTG** pentapeptide motif (Leu-Pro-X-Thr-Gly) and cleaves the peptide bond between threonine and glycine, forming a new **amide bond** between the threonine carboxyl and a poly-glycine nucleophile. *(3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Model 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** With neat reaction schemes, explain the NHS-ester and maleimide methods of enzyme activation. Discuss the role of buffer pH in each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the principle, procedure, and application of **Sortase A-mediated conjugation**. What are its advantages over random NHS-ester chemistry? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a)

**NHS-ester activation:**
- The NHS-ester reagent (e.g., sulfo-SMCC, BS³, NHS-PEG) reacts with the ε-amine of lysine residues.
- Reaction occurs at **pH 7.0 – 9.0**, where a significant fraction of lysines is deprotonated (free amine form).
- The NHS acts as a good leaving group, displacing and forming a stable **amide bond** between the protein and the label.

$$
Enzyme-NH_2 \;+\; R-CO-O-NHS \;\longrightarrow\; Enzyme-NH-CO-R \;+\; NHS-OH
$$

- Buffers like **PBS or HEPES** are preferred; **Tris and glycine are forbidden** because their free amines compete and consume the NHS-ester.

**Maleimide activation:**
- Maleimide reagents (e.g., SMCC, sulfo-SMCC, GMBS) react selectively with **thiols** (cysteine side chains).
- They undergo a Michael addition across the double bond of the maleimide ring, forming a stable **thioether bond**.
- The reaction is optimal at **pH 6.5 – 7.5** — at higher pH (>8.5), the reaction becomes less selective and the maleimide ring can hydrolyze or undergo retro-Michael reaction.

$$
Enzyme-SH \;+\; R-Mal \;\longrightarrow\; Enzyme-S-CH(R)-CH(CO-NH)-CO
$$

- Add **EDTA** to chelate trace metals, and work under **N₂/Ar** to prevent thiol oxidation to disulfides.

**Buffer pH roles:**

| Method | Optimal pH | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| NHS-ester | 7.0 – 9.0 | Lysine amine must be deprotonated |
| Maleimide | 6.5 – 7.5 | Thiol must be deprotonated; maleimide must remain unhydrolyzed |

*(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution for Part (b)

**Principle of Sortase A Conjugation:**
Sortase A is a 25 kDa *S. aureus* transpeptidase that recognizes the pentapeptide **LPXTG** motif near the C-terminus of a substrate protein. It cleaves the peptide bond between threonine and glycine via its active-site cysteine, forming a thioester-linked intermediate. A poly-glycine nucleophile (e.g., GGG-fluorophore) then attacks this intermediate, releasing Sortase and forming a new **amide bond** between the threonine carboxyl and the glycine amine.

**Procedure:**
1. Clone the protein of interest fused to **LPETG** (variant of LPXTG) at the C-terminus.
2. Express and purify the fusion protein.
3. Incubate with **Sortase A (5–50 µM)** and **GGG-conjugate (0.1–1 mM)** in 50 mM Tris, 150 mM NaCl, 10 mM CaCl₂, pH 7.5, at 4–25 °C for 1–4 h.
4. Optionally remove the His-tagged Sortase by Ni-NTA affinity chromatography.
5. Analyze by SDS-PAGE and mass spectrometry.

**Application:**
- Site-specific labeling of antibodies (ADC development).
- PEGylation of therapeutic proteins.
- Site-specific immobilization on surfaces.
- Construction of bispecific antibodies.

**Advantages over random NHS-ester chemistry:**
1. **Site-specific** — gives 1:1 homogeneous conjugate; NHS gives heterogeneous mixtures with variable DOL.
2. **Stable amide bond** — the resulting linkage is protease-resistant and non-reversible (vs. hydrazone from periodate).
3. **No loss of antigen binding** — conjugation occurs at a defined C-terminal site, away from antigen-binding paratopes.
4. **Defined pharmacokinetics** — crucial for ADCs (DAR = 2, 4, 6 known exactly).
5. **Mild conditions** — aqueous, neutral pH, ambient temperature; preserves protein folding.

*(7 marks)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Model 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** Explain periodate oxidation and glutaraldehyde methods of enzyme activation. Compare their selectivity. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the role of **EDC/NHS coupling** in activating carboxyl groups of enzymes. Give a worked example for immobilizing Glucose Oxidase (GOx) on a carboxylated gold electrode. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a)

**Periodate Oxidation:**
- Sodium periodate ($NaIO_4$) cleaves the **cis-diol bond** (C2–C3) of sugar residues (mannose, galactose, sialic acid) on glycoproteins, generating **two aldehyde groups** per diol.
- Reaction conditions: **1–10 mM NaIO₄, 4 °C, 30 min, in the dark**.
- The aldehydes couple to **hydrazide- or aminooxy-functionalized** partners forming **hydrazone** or **oxime** bonds; can be stabilized with **NaBH₃CN** reduction.
- Highly **selective for glycosylated enzymes** (e.g., HRP, GOx, many antibodies).
- Non-glycosylated proteins cannot be activated by this method.

$$
R-CH(OH)-CH(OH)-R' \;+\; NaIO_4 \;\longrightarrow\; R-CHO \;+\; R'-CHO
$$

**Glutaraldehyde Activation:**
- A bifunctional dialdehyde (**OHC-(CH₂)₃-CHO**) that reacts with **ε-amines of lysine** at both ends, forming **Schiff base linkages** (imine bonds).
- Reaction conditions: **0.5–2.5% glutaraldehyde, pH 7–9, room temperature, 1–2 h**.
- Often used for **enzyme immobilization on aminated supports** (e.g., aminopropyl glass, chitosan, amino beads).
- Schiff bases can be reduced to stable secondary amines using **NaBH₄ or NaBH₃CN**.

$$
Enzyme-NH_2 \;+\; OHC-R-CHO \;\longrightarrow\; Enzyme-N=CH-R-CHO \;\xrightarrow{Enzyme'-NH_2}\; Enzyme-N=CH-R-CH=N-Enzyme'
$$

**Comparison of Selectivity:**

| Feature | Periodate | Glutaraldehyde |
|---|---|---|
| Target group | Glycan cis-diols | Lysine ε-amines |
| Requires glycoprotein? | Yes | No |
| Number of sites | Limited to glycan positions | Many (all surface lysines) |
| Crosslinking risk | Low | High (self-polymerization of GA) |
| Resulting bond | Hydrazone / oxime (reducible to amine) | Schiff base (reducible to amine) |

*(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution for Part (b)

**Principle of EDC/NHS Coupling:**
- **1-Ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)carbodiimide (EDC)** is a zero-length crosslinker that activates **carboxyl groups (-COOH)** to form an **O-acylisourea** intermediate.
- **N-Hydroxysuccinimide (NHS)** or **sulfo-NHS** stabilizes this intermediate by forming a more stable **NHS-ester**, which then reacts with primary amines to form **amide bonds**.
- The reaction is performed in **MES buffer (0.1 M, pH 4.5–6.0)** for activation, then shifted to **PBS (pH 7.4)** for coupling.

$$
Enzyme-COOH \;+\; EDC \;\longrightarrow\; Enzyme-CO-O-C(NH-Et)-NHR \;+\; NHS \;\longrightarrow\; Enzyme-CO-O-NHS
$$
$$
Enzyme-CO-O-NHS \;+\; H_2N-Surface \;\longrightarrow\; Enzyme-CO-NH-Surface \;+\; NHS-OH
$$

**Worked Example: Immobilizing GOx on Carboxylated Gold Electrode**

**Step 1 — Activation of surface carboxyls:**
- Treat carboxylated SAM (e.g., 11-mercaptoundecanoic acid on gold) with **EDC (0.4 M)** and **sulfo-NHS (0.1 M)** in **0.1 M MES buffer (pH 5.5)** for **30 min at room temperature**.
- Wash with MES buffer.

**Step 2 — Coupling to GOx amines:**
- Incubate activated electrode in **1 mg/mL GOx in PBS (pH 7.4)** for **2 h at 4 °C**.
- GOx lysines attack the NHS-ester, forming amide bonds.
- Wash with PBS to remove unbound GOx.

**Step 3 — Quench residual NHS-esters:**
- Immerse electrode in **1 M ethanolamine (pH 8.5)** for **15 min**.

**Step 4 — Verification:**
- Cyclic voltammetry: scan from 0 to +0.6 V (vs. Ag/AgCl) in presence of glucose; observe catalytic H₂O₂ oxidation peak.
- Impedance spectroscopy: increase in charge-transfer resistance confirms enzyme immobilization.
- Activity assay: couple to HRP/o-dianisidine system; expect retention of ≥70% activity.

**Mass balance for planning:**

$$
n_{COOH} \;=\; \frac{m_{SAM} \cdot N_A}{MW_{SAM}} \quad ; \quad n_{EDC} \;=\; 10 \times n_{COOH}
$$

*(7 marks)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU 2024 Scheme Bioconjugate Papers:**
> 1. **Buffer choice error** — Many students write "use Tris buffer for NHS coupling" — this is WRONG. Tris is a primary amine and competes with protein amines, killing the reaction. Use PBS or HEPES. *Loss: 1–2 marks.*
> 2. **Confusing Sortase A and mTG** — Sortase A recognizes **LPXTG** and requires a poly-G nucleophile. mTG recognizes **Q-tag** and **K-tag** and forms an **isopeptide** bond. Do not interchange.
> 3. **Writing "Schiff base" without reduction** — Glutaraldehyde/periodate-derived Schiff bases are reversible; examiners expect you to mention **NaBH₃CN or NaBH₄ reduction** for stability.
> 4. **Ignoring DOL** — Conjugation quality is judged by DOL; failing to define or calculate it loses 2–3 marks.
> 5. **Forgetting activity check** — Activation may denature the enzyme; always include a final activity assay step (e.g., TMB for HRP, pNPP for AP, glucose assay for GOx).
> 6. **Confusing N-hydroxysuccinimide with N-hydroxysulfosuccinimide** — sulfo-NHS is water-soluble; NHS is not. Mention solubility if asked.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Activated Enzymes for Conjugation**

- **Definition:** Activated enzymes are enzymes with reactive handles (NHS, maleimide, aldehyde, azide, alkyne, biotin, LPXTG, Q-tag) installed for covalent bioconjugation.
- **Two big families:** (1) **Chemical activation** (NHS, maleimide, periodate, glutaraldehyde, EDC), (2) **Enzymatic activation** (Sortase A, mTG, BirA, SNAP/CLIP/HaloTag).
- **NHS-ester → Lysine** (pH 7–9); **Maleimide → Cysteine** (pH 6.5–7.5).
- **Periodate → Glycoprotein glycans**; **Glutaraldehyde → Lysines**; **EDC/NHS → Carboxyls**.
- **Avoid amine buffers** (Tris, glycine) when using NHS or EDC.
- **Sortase A recognition:** LPXTG, forms **amide bond** with poly-G nucleophile, thioester intermediate.
- **mTG recognition:** Q-tag + K-tag, forms **isopeptide bond**, Ca²⁺-independent (microbial).
- **BirA tag:** GLNDIFEAQKIEWHE, ATP-dependent, biotin ligase.
- **DOL formula:** $DOL = (A_{dye}/\varepsilon_{dye}) / ((A_{280} - A_{dye}\cdot CF)/\varepsilon_{protein})$.
- **Always include:** buffer choice, pH, temperature, quench step, purification step, activity verification.
- **Industrial applications:** ELISA kits, ADC therapeutics, glucose biosensors, PEGylated biocatalysts.
- **Storage of activated conjugates:** -20 °C / -80 °C in PBS + 50% glycerol, protected from light.
- **Stability ranking of bonds (most → least stable):** Amide > Thioether > Triazole > Hydrazone (reduced) > Schiff base (unreduced) > Disulfide.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module 3
